Description: Managing package version constraints in pubspec.yaml for predictable builds
Do: Use caret syntax (^) for compatible versions; pin exact versions for critical deps; run pub upgrade regularly
Dont: Use >= without upper bound; pin everything preventing security patches
Example:
# Good: caret syntax allows compatible updates
dependencies:
  flutter_bloc: ^8.1.0  # Allows 8.1.0 to 8.x.x
  dio: ^5.3.0           # Allows 5.3.0 to 5.x.x
  freezed_annotation: ^2.4.0

# Pin critical dependencies
dependency_overrides:
  crypto: 3.0.3  # Pinned for known working version
Severity:
# Bad: too loose
dependencies:
  dio: '>=5.0.0'  # Could get breaking changes
  http: any        # Completely unpredictable

Description: Managing pubspec.lock for reproducible builds across team and CI
Do: Commit pubspec.lock for apps; don't commit for packages; review lock file changes
Dont: Ignore lock file for apps; commit lock file for published packages
Example:
# For apps (commit lock file)
git add pubspec.lock
git commit -m 'Update dependencies'

# For packages (.gitignore)
# pubspec.lock  # Don't commit - consumers resolve their own

# CI: use --enforce-lockfile
flutter pub get --enforce-lockfile
Severity: # .gitignore (BAD for apps)\npubspec.lock  # Different versions on every team member's machine

Description: Regularly auditing dependencies for security vulnerabilities and updates
Do: Run flutter pub outdated regularly; check pub.dev scores; review changelogs before upgrading
Dont: Ignore outdated warnings; upgrade without reading changelogs; use packages with low pub.dev scores
Example:
# Check outdated
flutter pub outdated

# Check for specific issues
flutter pub deps

# Upgrade all (respecting constraints)
flutter pub upgrade

# Upgrade to latest major versions
flutter pub upgrade --major-versions

# Audit script in CI
flutter pub outdated --json | jq '.packages[] | select(.upgradable != null)'

Description: Managing iOS native dependencies via CocoaPods or Swift Package Manager
Do: Keep Podfile clean; run pod install after changes; commit Podfile.lock; specify deployment target
Dont: Edit Pods/ directory manually; ignore pod install errors; use incompatible pod versions
Example:
# Podfile best practices
platform :ios, '14.0'

target 'Runner' do
  use_frameworks!
  
  # Explicit pod versions for reproducibility
  pod 'FirebaseCore', '~> 10.0'
  pod 'GoogleMaps', '~> 8.0'
end

post_install do |installer|
  installer.pods_project.targets.each do |target|
    target.build_configurations.each do |config|
      config.build_settings['IPHONEOS_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET'] = '14.0'
    end
  end
end

# Commands
cd ios && pod install --repo-update
Severity: # Editing files in Pods/ directory directly\n# Ignoring Podfile.lock\n# Not running pod install after pubspec changes

Description: Managing Android native dependencies via Gradle
Do: Use implementation instead of compile; keep gradle versions updated; use version catalogs
Dont: Use deprecated compile; mix Kotlin and Java dependencies without thought; ignore Gradle warnings

Description: Properly separating development-time from runtime dependencies
Do: Put testing and codegen packages in dev_dependencies; never import dev packages in lib/
Dont: Mix dev and runtime dependencies; include test packages in regular dependencies
Example:
# pubspec.yaml
dependencies:
  flutter_bloc: ^8.1.0
  dio: ^5.3.0
  freezed_annotation: ^2.4.0
  json_annotation: ^4.8.0

dev_dependencies:
  flutter_test:
    sdk: flutter
  build_runner: ^2.4.0
  freezed: ^2.4.0
  json_serializable: ^6.7.0
  mocktail: ^1.0.0
  bloc_test: ^9.1.0
  flutter_lints: ^3.0.0
Severity:
# BAD: test/codegen packages in regular deps
dependencies:
  mocktail: ^1.0.0  # Shipped to users!
  build_runner: ^2.4.0  # Not needed at runtime

Description: Managing multiple packages in a monorepo with Melos for shared dependencies
Do: Use Melos for workspace management; share common deps via workspace; use path dependencies for internal packages
Dont: Manually manage each package; duplicate shared code; publish internal packages unnecessarily
Example:
# melos.yaml
name: my_workspace
packages:
  - packages/**
  - apps/**

command:
  bootstrap:
    usePubspecOverrides: true

scripts:
  analyze:
    run: melos exec -- flutter analyze
  test:
    run: melos exec -- flutter test

# Internal package dependency
# packages/app/pubspec.yaml
dependencies:
  shared_ui:
    path: ../shared_ui
Severity: # Separate repos for shared code\n# Copy-pasting between packages\n# Publishing internal-only packages to pub.dev

Description: Setting up dependency injection properly with registration order and scoping
Do: Register in correct order (data -> domain -> presentation); use lazy singletons; dispose when needed
Dont: Register everything eagerly; create circular dependencies; use manual registration for large projects

Description: Resolving version conflicts between transitive dependencies of different packages
Do: Use dependency_overrides sparingly; check compatibility before upgrading; pin conflicting versions
Dont: Force override without understanding implications; ignore resolution errors
Example:
# Check dependency tree
flutter pub deps

# If conflict exists
# pubspec.yaml
dependency_overrides:
  collection: 1.18.0  # Force specific version for compatibility
  
# Better: find compatible versions
# flutter pub outdated shows which versions work together

# Last resort: fork and fix
# git dependency
dependencies:
  problematic_package:
    git:
      url: https://github.com/yourfork/package
      ref: fix-compatibility
Severity:
# Ignoring the error
# OR overriding without testing
dependency_overrides:
  http: 1.0.0  # May break packages that need http 0.13.x

Description: Choosing and managing Flutter SDK channels for stability and features
Do: Use stable for production; beta for testing upcoming features; master only for contributing
Dont: Use master channel in production; switch channels without testing; skip SDK versions
Example:
# Check current channel
flutter channel

# Switch to stable
flutter channel stable
flutter upgrade

# Use FVM for per-project versions
fvm use stable
fvm use 3.16.0  # Specific version

# CI: pin SDK version
# .fvmrc
{
   lutter": "3.16.0"
Docs URL:
# Using master in production
flutter channel master
# Breaking changes surprise!

# Different versions across team
# Dev A: 3.16.0, Dev B: 3.13.0 -> issues

Description: Ensuring Flutter plugin versions are compatible with native platform requirements
Do: Check plugin changelogs for native SDK version requirements; test on real devices after upgrades
Dont: Upgrade plugins without checking native compatibility; ignore platform-specific breaking changes
Example:
# Before upgrading
# 1. Check CHANGELOG.md
# 2. Check minimum iOS/Android versions
# 3. Check native SDK requirements

# Example: firebase_core upgrade
# Requires: iOS 13.0+, Android API 21+
# Requires: Xcode 15+
# Requires: CocoaPods 1.12+

# Verify after upgrade
flutter clean
cd ios && pod install --repo-update
flutter run  # Test on real device
Severity:
# Blind upgrade
flutter pub upgrade --major-versions
# Then wonder why iOS build fails
# 'Minimum deployment target is 14.0 but plugin requires 15.0'

Description: Managing Gradle wrapper version for consistent Android builds
Do: Commit gradle-wrapper.properties and gradlew; upgrade Gradle wrapper with defined process
Dont: Edit gradle-wrapper.jar manually; skip Gradle wrapper version upgrades
Example:
# Upgrade Gradle wrapper
cd android
./gradlew wrapper --gradle-version 8.4

# gradle/wrapper/gradle-wrapper.properties
distributionUrl=https\://services.gradle.org/distributions/gradle-8.4-bin.zip

# Keep in sync with Android Gradle Plugin
# AGP 8.2 requires Gradle 8.2+
# plugins { id 'com.android.application' version '8.2.0' }
Severity: # Different gradle versions per developer\n# Not committing gradlew to VCS\n# Using incompatible AGP + Gradle versions
